// FILE TARGET: src/network.zig
// CODE DIFF CONTEXT:
@@ -1441,6 +1465,9 @@ pub const Connection = struct { // MARK: Connection
 	secureChannel: SecureChannel,
 	slowChannel: Channel,
 
+	restartChannelCounter: [3]u32 = .{0, 0, 0},
+	restartCounter: u32 = 0,

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
But you can use overflow arithmetic.

> There is no good reason to shorten this variable, just to save a few bytes where it doesn't matter.

It takes more space in the struct. You are making it 12 bytes bigger for no reason.
Of course it probably won't have any measurable impact in any benchmark, but it could be the 12 bytes that push another field into a new cache line. I don't any reason to make it worse, especially since both implementations would be equally complex.

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
```suggestion

```

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
As already mentioned on the other PR #2055, please use `.Display = .{key array}, .Gamplay = .{key array}, ...`

To make this usable in the other functions you should first preprocess this into a list or hashmap at compile time.
Something like

```zig
pub var keys: []const *Window.Key = .{
     comptime var size: usize = 0;
     inline for(structuredKeys) |array| {
         size += array.len
     }
     comptime var result: [size]*Window.Key = undefined;
     inline for(structuredKeys) |array| {
         @memcpy(...);
     }
     return &result;
};
```

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
I tried something like this, as well as many other ways, and every other way was even more of a mess, primarily because of the differently-sized arrays. This was the cleanest and most readable solution I could find.

I can try again when i get home after the weekend though.

Do I understand you correctly that you want to add some options on the struct that would control what the press event would do? For example, add pointer to boolean like ` .toggle=&gui.hideGui`?

Or maybe I misunderstand?

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
It might make sense to also pass a velocity in it, so it would not really make sense to always normalize it.

Either way that would be premature optimization, since handling normalized coordinates requires adding a lot more code and it's likely that this will not be called very often.
Switching to f32 is just "non-pessimization" to put it in Casey Muratori's terms. (Maybe I should add a section about this to the contributing guidelines?)

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
Yes, this is a crazy hack. I don't like hacks, hacks are not good for readability. I created a similar code snippet in [Compiler Explorer](https://godbolt.org/z/bhqePGMc1) and there both versions produce same assembly:

current:

```asm
example.registerAll__anon_4162:
        push    rbp
        mov     rbp, rsp
        sub     rsp, 416
        mov     qword ptr [rbp - 400], rdi
        mov     qword ptr [rbp - 392], rdi
        lea     rax, [rbp - 384]
```

// FILE TARGET: src/chunk.zig
// CODE DIFF CONTEXT:
@@ -108,6 +108,24 @@ pub const Neighbor = enum(u3) { // MARK: Neighbor
 			},
 		}
 	}
+
+	// Returns the neighbor that is rotated by 90 degrees counterclockwise around the x axis.
+	pub inline fn rotateX(self: Neighbor) Neighbor {

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
Depends if we cache rotated structures. 

---

If we do, with palettes only on IO we will perform:
- 1 translation (`O(xyz)` complexity, X, Y, Z are dimensions of structure), 
- N pastes (`O(xyz)` complexity),
- up to 1+1+1 rotations (90, 180, 270 degrees) (single 90 degree rotation has `O(xyz)` complexity since rotations are O(1) as we use lookup tables for most of them)

This is `O((N+4)*xyz)`.

---

If we don't cache once rotated structures them we perform:

- 1 translation `O(xyz)`
- N pastes `O(xyz)`
- up to A+2B+3C rotations where A+B+C=N `O(xyz)`

Which yields `O((N+1)*xyz + 2*N*xyz) = O((3*N+1)*xyz)` 

(I assume we get similar number of 0, 90, 180 an 270 degree rotations, hence second 2*N*xyz).

---

If we use palette with no cache we get:

- N translations `O(xyz)`
- N pastes `O(xyz)`
- A+2B+3C rotations where A+B+C=N `O(xyz)`

Which yields `O(2*N*xyz + 2*N*xyz) = O(4*N*xyz)`

---

If we get palette with cache we get:

- N translations `O(xyz)`
- N pastes `O(xyz)`
- up to `1+1+1` rotations

Which yields `O((2*N+3)*xyz)`

Best time complexity (if i didn't F anything up) is still no palette with cache for rotated structures.

@@ -0,0 +1,88 @@
+const std = @import("std");

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
This lake has a bunch of bugs:
Caves can be seen through the bottom of a lake:
![Screenshot at 2024-06-03 11-31-56](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/43880493/c310b7a0-703c-488f-a5ea-a730f0a89bda)
The lake interacts badly with other structures:
![Screenshot at 2024-06-03 11-55-34](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/43880493/c7f98265-d418-4445-9f4e-07d05a4a8e22)
![Screenshot at 2024-06-03 11-55-49](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/43880493/2f27949a-8d05-4862-9322-f38b4e904ced)
![Screenshot at 2024-06-03 11-56-00](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/43880493/ff2df8ce-e3c9-4967-830a-e9afe640469a)
The lake itself sometimes cuts off at a chunk border (note that this is not #203, since it breaks close to the center of the lake):

![Screenshot at 2024-06-03 11-57-04](https://github.com/PixelGuys/Cubyz/assets/43880493/694b39df-632c-4730-b42a-75d4b8faf62e)


I think it might be better to do structures like this during map generation. This would require some more work though, like introducing a new structure system for MapGen structures.

// FILE TARGET: src/main.zig
// CODE DIFF CONTEXT:
@@ -324,6 +324,8 @@ pub fn setNextKeypressListener(listener: ?*const fn(c_int, c_int, c_int) void) !
 fn escape() void {
 	if(game.world == null) return;
 	Window.setMouseGrabbed(!Window.grabbed);
+
+	gui.openWindow("pause_menu");

// CRITICAL ARCHITECTURAL REVIEW:
This can be quite intrusive. The escape key has more functionalities than opening the pause menu.
It can also be used to just open the inventory (when it has been opened before)
`I` - open inventory
`Escape` - return to game
`Escape` - return to inventory
→ "Oh no the pause menu is now open, but I just wanted to return to the the inventory"
And even worse the pause menu also appears when you do this:
`I` - open inventory
`Escape` - return to game
`C` - open creative menu
→ "Oh no the pause menu is now open as well."

I think an approach like Terraria might be better. There pressing escape opens the inventory and other guis, but there is a small gear in the bottom right corner. Clicking on that opens the pause menu.
The gear would be a separate window with just a single (icon) button that opens the actual pause_menu. I'd recommend to set the following options for this window:
`.isHud = true,` → will always be open in the game
`.showTitleBar = false,`
`.hideIfMouseIsGrabbed = false,` → only shows the button when the cursor is free
